skill.name: performance-analysis
skill.description: Analyzes Claude Flow swarm performance, detects bottlenecks, and recommends optimization steps for reports and monitoring.
skill.summary: Detect bottlenecks, profile swarm activity, and generate performance reports for Claude Flow workflows.
skill.author: Claude Flow Team

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command execution and filesystem indicators in SKILL.md. Review shows these are mostly documentation examples for Claude Flow performance commands. The skill still carries medium operational risk because it recommends external command execution, local file writes, and automatic remediation. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, obfuscation, or confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: External command execution guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs users or agents to run Claude Flow commands and includes a Node.js child_process exec example. This appears legitimate for performance analysis, but it can execute external package code in the user environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution patterns are explicit and numerous, including child_process exec. The commands are fixed Claude Flow examples, so the evidence supports operational risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Automatic remediation can modify runtime behavior
medium_findings.description: The documentation recommends an automatic fix mode and describes topology, caching, concurrency, priority, and resource changes. These operations may alter a user workflow and should require explicit user approval before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The --fix option and listed optimization actions are directly documented. The exact implementation is outside this skill file, so the risk depends on the installed Claude Flow tool.

medium_findings.title: Local report export and file write examples
medium_findings.description: The skill documents report export paths and a script that writes a combined analysis file. This is expected for reporting, but users should constrain output paths to avoid overwriting important local files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem writes are clear and local. They use fixed documentation paths, so the concern is bounded unless a user changes paths or automates them unsafely.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography findings are false positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography matches occur in descriptive performance text and a sample severity field. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or other weak cryptographic algorithm use in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are frontmatter description text and a JSON output example. They do not contain cryptographic code or cryptographic API calls.

low_findings.title: Path traversal findings are documentation links
low_findings.description: The ../ path indicators appear only in See Also links to neighboring skill documentation. They are not used as executable filesystem input or file access logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is limited to markdown links. No runtime path resolution or file read operation is present at those lines.

low_findings.title: Hidden file access findings are documentation links
low_findings.description: The .claude indicators appear in markdown links to guide files. They do not instruct the skill to read hidden files or collect hidden configuration data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are static documentation references. There is no command or script at those lines that opens hidden files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Node.js child_process exec example
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a JavaScript helper that executes shell commands through child_process exec. The example uses fixed Claude Flow commands, but this pattern can become command injection if changed to include untrusted input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exec call is directly present in the sample script. The current command strings are fixed, which lowers confidence for exploitable injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell loop with command substitution
dangerous_patterns.description: The continuous monitoring example uses a shell loop and date-based command substitution for report filenames. This is normal operational shell usage, but it can run indefinitely if copied without controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The loop and command substitution are explicit in the documentation. The example does not include untrusted input or network exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Synchronous local file write
dangerous_patterns.description: The JavaScript example writes a combined performance report to a local analysis path. This is a legitimate report action, but it should be limited to user-approved workspace locations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem write is directly shown. It targets a fixed local path and does not show traversal or secret collection.

content.value_statement: Claude Flow swarms can slow down when agents, memory, or coordination are not balanced. This skill guides bottleneck detection, profiling, reporting, and optimization planning.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to run Claude Flow bottleneck detection commands.
Documents performance report generation in markdown, HTML, and JSON formats.
Lists metrics for communication, processing, memory, and network bottlenecks.
Provides examples for MCP-based bottleneck and task result analysis.
Describes optimization areas such as topology, caching, concurrency, and scheduling.
Shows CI and continuous monitoring patterns for performance reports.
limitations: Requires a working Claude Flow environment and the related command-line tools.
Does not include executable implementation code beyond documentation examples.
Automatic fixes depend on the behavior of the installed Claude Flow package.
Reported improvement percentages are examples, not guaranteed results.

use_cases.title: Diagnose slow swarm workflows
use_cases.description: Find coordination, memory, processing, and network bottlenecks after a Claude Flow run.

use_cases.title: Create performance reports for releases
use_cases.description: Generate repeatable performance summaries for pull requests, releases, or weekly reviews.

use_cases.title: Tune agent topology and caching
use_cases.description: Use reported bottlenecks to plan safer changes to topology, cache use, and concurrency.

prompt_templates.title: Run a basic bottleneck check
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner analysis for the current swarm
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to help me check the current Claude Flow swarm for major performance bottlenecks. Ask before running commands.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a performance report
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to prepare a performance report for the last 24 hours. Include key metrics, bottlenecks, and recommended next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Compare performance after a change
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to compare current swarm performance with a previous run. Highlight regressions, improvements, and likely causes.

prompt_templates.title: Design a monitoring workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a safe continuous performance monitoring process for Claude Flow. Include approval points for any automatic fixes.

output_examples.input: Check why my Claude Flow swarm is slow.
output_examples.output: A short bottleneck summary that separates communication, processing, memory, and network issues, followed by prioritized recommendations.

output: Executive summary with the main performance score.
Trend notes for task time, agent utilization, cache hit rate, and parallel efficiency.
Recommended changes with expected impact and review priority.

output_examples.input: Plan safe optimization changes for a busy swarm.
output_examples.output: A staged plan that starts with measurement, then tests topology, caching, and concurrency changes before applying fixes.

best_practices: Review generated commands before execution, especially commands with --fix or output paths.
Create a baseline report before changing topology, cache settings, or concurrency.
Store reports in a dedicated workspace directory with clear retention rules.
anti_patterns: Do not run automatic fixes in production without a backup or approval step.
Do not compare reports from different workloads as if they are equivalent.
Do not treat sample improvement percentages as guaranteed performance gains.

faq.question: What does this skill analyze?
faq.answer: It covers Claude Flow swarm bottlenecks across communication, processing, memory, network, and coordination behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands by itself?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation. An AI assistant may propose commands, but users should approve execution first.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes command execution, automatic fix guidance, loops, and local file writes. These are useful but require care.

faq.question: Can it generate HTML or JSON reports?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented Claude Flow commands include markdown, HTML, and JSON report formats.

faq.question: Can it compare two swarm runs?
faq.answer: Yes. The report options include comparison with a previous swarm or run identifier.

faq.question: What should I review before using --fix?
faq.answer: Review the target environment, expected changes, rollback plan, and whether the installed Claude Flow package is trusted.
